```

proj/__main__.py
proj/app.py

I've got a subclass of `Worker` called `Application` in `proj/app.py`. I've got a service `DiscoveryService` located in `proj/services/discovery.py`. if I configure **DEBUG** mode in `Application`, it doesn't apply to the logger in `DiscoveryService`.

I believe this is simply because of Python's cascading logging - meaning, I need to set the logger in `proj/__init__.py` to address this. logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

from . import logger as module_logger

class Application(mode.Worker):
    ...

    def on_setup_root_logger(self, _logger, _loglevel):

```
Assuming I'm on the right path, filling in that "what to do here" bit is where I'm confused. I'm sure it's simple though. 🙄

issue_title: Add support for asyncio to client
issue_body:
Python 3.4 adds support for [asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html), a native model for concurrent processing. This library should adopt it as one option for the event loop.

Some work has been done in the [asyncio branch](/jaraco/irc/tree/asyncio).

What remains is to identify what shortcomings exist, test the implementation, and provide directions on how to use this feature.

issue_title: Viterbi 算法得到的是类似贪心算法的局部最优？
issue_body:
Wiki 上的 Python 算法针对的是又两个隐藏状态的特例，所以可以像贪心一样从 起点开始，不断向前选择，每次选择都没有后效性。

现在对于分词的问题，有四个隐藏状态，那么这个时候的 Viterbi 算法 要得到全局最优解应该只能从从后往前回溯得到。如果从前往后，每一步选择局部最优，得到的结果不是全局最优。

英文维基上的伪代码是全局最优的一个通用实现，Python 的不是。

在维基参考文献上看到的一个比较清晰的[介绍](http://www.kanungo.com/software/hmmtut.pdf) 24页。

![Viterbi 算法截图](http://wertion.github.io/pic/hmmtut_%E9%A1%B5%E9%9D%A2_24.png) 

我的一些理解哈，也可能不对，麻烦大家看一下吧。

    if stock.startswith("009") or stock.startswith("126") or stock.startswith("110") or stock.startswith(

    return 0
```

issue_title: Question : Breaking of paired-end chains
issue_body:
Hi @lh3,

  I am very intrigued by the way minimap2 handles paired-end short reads --- the solution seems quite elegant.  According to the paper:

>  During chaining, minimap2 takes a pair of reads as one fragment with a gap of unknown length in the middle. It applies a normal gap cost between seeds on the same read but is a more permissive gap cost between seeds on different reads. ... After identifying primary chains (Section 2.1.3), we split each fragment chain into two read chains and perform alignment for each read as in Section 2.2. 

I am curious if there is a place I can find more details about how the resulting chain is split into 2 read chains.  I believe the code to do that is [here](https://github.com/lh3/minimap2/blob/master/hit.c#L348), but I'm not certain.  In most cases, one would imagine that there is only one "transition" from the read1 chain to the read 2 chain.  However, if the paired-end reads are overlapping, then there might be multiple transitions between the hits for the different reads in the optimal chain, right?  In this case, how does minimap2 split the chain for the pair into 2 separate (consistent) read chains where each contains only hits from a single read?  Any insight you could provide into that algorithm would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks!
Rob

issue_title: Terminal prompt takes forever to appear. eval $(thefuck --alias)
issue_body:
zsh
The Fuck 3.24 using Python 3.6.2
OS: Arch Linux x86_64 Kernel Release: 4.13.9-1-ARCH
Ok, so I'm not sure if this is a bug or if I've done something wrong. I noticed that when opening any terminal, the prompt would take about 10 to 15 secs before it appeared. I eventually found this in my ~/.zshrc under:

```
# alias
alias pacupg='sudo pacman -Syu'
alias ls='ls --color=auto -lh'
alias l='ls'
echo 'eval $(thefuck --alias)' >> ~/.zshrc

# run ls after directory change
function chpwd() {
    emulate -L zsh
    ls
}

eval $(thefuck --alias)

```
There were about a hundred of these, **eval $(thefuck --alias)** entries at the bottom of zshrc
I removed them and when opening a terminal window, everything is instantaneous, no lag at all. So after saving the config and exiting, proceed to open another terminal and for every subsequent terminal window that I open it adds one more of these lines and obviously after a while when i've opened a few hundred of these terminal windows over a period of time I have a few hundred of these entries in my **zshrc**.
To stop this behaviour (as a temporary measure) I commented out the line  **echo 'eval $(thefuck --alias)' >> ~/.zshrc** but presume that is not the correct way to go about it. I'm definitely a rookie when it comes to these things so apologies if I've gone about this in the wrong way, here and on my PC!

issue_title: Add support for custom fields
issue_body:
- Go to zendesk and add a custom field in your ticket (admin > ticket fields > add custom field)
- Add a custom field and copy-paste the ID
- If you now go to the ticket view you will be able to see new fields on the left-hand side of the ticket.

You can now fill this custom field at Ticket creation by doing this API call:

```bash
curl https://[SUBDOMAIN].zendesk.com/api/v2/tickets.json -d '{"ticket": { "custom_fields": [{"id": [CUSTOM FIELD ID], "value": "HELLO"}], "requester": {"name": "John Doe", "email": "john@domain.com"}, "subject": "This is the subject!", "comment": { "body": "This is the question." }}}' -H "Content-Type: application/json" -v -u [EMAIL ADDRESS]/token:[TOKEN] -X POST
```

It would be great if zenpy supported this, perhaps as:

```python
Ticket(custom_fields=[CustomField(id=1234, value="Hello", CustomField(id=1235, value=15), CustomField(id=1236, value=datetime.now()], ...)
```

Custom fields can be strings, numbers or dates.

It does seem that it takes a while for custom fields to become "active" in the API. I.e. there is some delay between creating a custom field and actually being able to POST it via the API...

issue_title: Fetcher uses too much memory
issue_body:
* pyspider version: latest commit on master (14th of June, 2018) `3fb9167983f0123b9cac6615e543e9fbaf68cc04`
* Operating system: debian stretch in a docker container

I am using pyspider deployed with a similar docker-compose file as shown [in the doc](http://docs.pyspider.org/en/latest/Running-pyspider-with-Docker/)

I have configured a lot of projects : a bit more than 200 and I recrawl most of them every 6hours to check for news pages.
It works pretty well.
But I noticed that the container for the fetcher service uses a lot memory,and does not releases it even when it has no task to fetch.
When every task is completed, the container for each services uses less than 200MiB of memory, except for the fetcher container which uses more than 2Gib !
It became an issue because I have other apps running on the same server also using a significant amount of memory and it led to the crash of the fetcher container (and therefore the entire process of pyspider).

I first tried to limit the memory usage of the fetcher container using docker, but when the fetcher reached that limit it stopped working (not processing any task)
I tried to configure 2 fetcher container, but it just doubled the memory usage.

I then suspected tornado to have memory leak issues. I managed to adapt the tornado_fetcher code to use tornado in it's version 5.1 and built my own docker image with tornado 5.1 installed. The crawl goes well but I couldn't find significant improvement in the memory usage. However the debug mode of the webui does not work due to tornado breaking changes and I couldn't fix it.

I guess that some objects created during the fetching of a web page aren't removed by the garbage collector once the fetching is completed.
The fetcher uses more memory when crawling new pages but not when recrawling, i suppose that those objects can be reused by the fetcher on a new crawl. But due to the large amounts of pages i want to crawl I would have prefer the fetcher to free memory when the crawl of a project is done (after on_finished)

Have you met the same issue ?
How can I fix this ?

issue_title: Issues with notFilter
issue_body:
If one of `NamedTypes().__namedTypes` is `notFilter`, then `getTagMap()` fails due to `nt.getType().getTagMap()` being `None`. Is it `pyasn1`'s issue or `ldap3`'s one?

`nt.getType()` returns `ldap3.protocol.rfc4511.Not` instance which is impossible to print with Python 3.5:
```py
  File "/Users/andrei/.pyenv/versions/ldap/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyasn1/type/namedtype.py", line 148, in getTagMap
    print(nt.getType())
  File "/Users/andrei/.pyenv/versions/ldap/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 452, in __str__
    def __str__(self): return self._value.decode(self._encoding, 'ignore')
  File "/Users/andrei/.pyenv/versions/ldap/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyasn1/type/base.py", line 57, in __getattr__
    raise error.PyAsn1Error('No value for %s()' % attr)
pyasn1.error.PyAsn1Error: No value for decode()
```

#### Update:
`Not().getTagMap` is inherited from `Choice` and runs `Set.getComponentTagMap(self)` (due to empty `Not()._tagSet`):
```py
    def getComponentTagMap(self):
        if self._componentType:
            return self._componentType.getTagMap(True)
```
where `Not()._componentType` is empty so it returns `None` and fails later in various places.

issue_title: SVM threaded cross validation trainer return type inconsistent and fails to compile
issue_body:
The return value of cross_validate_trainer is 
`const matrix<double, 1, 2, typename trainer_type::mem_manager_type>`

The return value of cross_validate_trainer_threaded is
`const matrix<typename trainer_type::scalar_type, 1, 2, typename trainer_type::mem_manager_type>`

Note that the value type of the matrix switched from `double` to `trainer_type::scalar_type`.  This is confusing as I expected the threaded version to be a drop-in replacement for the unthreaded version with the exception of the number of threads argument.  

Even worse, the threaded version fails to compile if `trainer_type::scalar_type` is not `double`.  I do not mind the return type being double even if `trainer_type::scalar_type` is something else (like `float`), but it should compile.  The error is a compile time assert from matrix/matrix.h line 1474 that the assignment has incompatible types.  The error message references svm/svm_threaded.h line 60.  

I think the issues is that `cvtti_helpers::task::operator()` is instantiated with `matrix_type` = `matrix<trainer_type::scalar_type, 1, 2, trainer_type::mem_manager_type>`.  See svm/svm_threaded.h lines 55, 140, and 211.  Unfortunately, the return type of `test_binary_decision_function` is `const matrix<double,1,2> test_binary_decision_function`.  This causes an assignment  at svm/svm_threaded.h line 60 from a `matrix<double,1,2>` to a `matrix<trainer_type::scalar_type,1,2>`.  

It would see like changing the return type of cross_validate_trainer_threaded to `matrix<double,1,2>` and making the corresponding changes to cvtti_helpers::task would solve both issues.  

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/svm/svm.h#L536-L547

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/svm/svm_threaded.h#L227-L239

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/matrix/matrix.h#L1472-L1475

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/svm/svm_threaded.h#L48-L60

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/svm/svm_threaded.h#L140

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/38a2846ddad5a81fab9905b76d061ad473001eba/dlib/svm/svm.h#L160-L169

issue_title: decoding a payload that is a list
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using pyjwt with an api that accepts and returns jwt tokens.  I ran across a case where the api returned a token where the payload string encoded a list rather than a map, and the pyjwt decode() function resulted in "jwt.exceptions.DecodeError: Invalid payload string: must be a json object". 

see https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/master/jwt/api_jwt.py#L84-L85

 I'm wondering if encoding a list in the payload string violates the JWT protocol, and if not, whether there is (or should be) a way to handle this in pyjwt.  At the least, the error message seems misleading since if json.loads() is successful, I would think the string should count as a json object.

thanks

issue_title: Provide more detailed exceptions
issue_body:
Currently only a general `ApiError` exception is thrown, which can then be examined for a more detailed explanation of the error (e.g. the HTTP status code). It would be best to create sub classes of `ApiError` for the different known error conditions (e.g. 409 Conflic, 404 Not Found).

see http://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/API_v0.6 for a more detailed lists of errors.

This change should not break existing code, as the new exceptions classes can be implemented as sub classes of the existing `ApiError`.

issue_title: Memory usage grow on task switching
issue_body:
``` python
import asyncio

from PyQt5 import QtWidgets
from quamash import QEventLoop


@asyncio.coroutine
def Switcher():

    while True:

        yield from asyncio.sleep(0.001)


if __name__ == "__main__":

    import sys

    app = QtWidgets.QApplication(sys.argv)
    loop = QEventLoop(app)
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    with loop:

        loop.run_until_complete(Switcher())

```
Usage grow proportinally to switching speed. I made some big app with awesome :+1:  `quamash`, and saw slow growing for hours of work.

Tested with:
* Ubuntu 14.04 / Python 3.4.3 / PyQt 5.5
* Windows 7 / Python 3.5.0 / PyQt 5.5.1
* Windows xp / Python 3.4.4 / PyQt 5.5

issue_title: Incompatibility with Python 3
issue_body:
Hi,
see https://github.com/jsocol/pystatsd/blob/ace112e95d7f7231a87513622913bb77debe0d7d/statsd/client.py#L84  
Unfortunately `__metaclass__` has no effect in Python 3. The correct way in Python3 is: `class StatsClientBase(metaclass=abc.ABCMeta):` 

The best way how to keep compatibility with the Python 2 is to use `six` or `future.utils`. For only this particular purpose I recommend the `future.utils`. See http://python-future.org/_modules/future/utils.html#with_metaclass

issue_title: Python process hangs indefinitely when error is raised
issue_body:
If I start an SSH tunnel and some code crashes before `server.stop()` is called, then my python process hangs indefinitely. I'm get this behavior on Ubuntu 16.04 running Python 2.7.12 as well as OS X 10.12.6 running Python 2.7.15, both running sshtunnel v 0.1.2 from `pip install sshtunnel`. I also tested v0.1.4 that I cloned today from this repository into a fresh virtual environment on OS X. It hangs in all cases.

Minimal example:
```
from sshtunnel import SSHTunnelForwarder
server = SSHTunnelForwarder(
    'hostname.com',
    ssh_username="user",
    ssh_pkey="/path/to/id_rsa",
    remote_bind_address=('127.0.0.1', 5432)
)
server.start()
print(server.local_bind_port)  # show assigned local port
raise ValueError('random error')

server.stop()
```

When I run the example, I get:
```
$ python sshtunnel_then_error.py 
45528
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "foo.py", line 12, in <module>
    raise ValueError('random error')
ValueError: random error
```
At which point the program hangs indefinitely. 

I tried working around with atexit, but to no avail. Snippets from that experiment:
```
import atexit
def _cleanup():
    global server
    try:
        server.stop()
    except:
        pass
atexit.register(_cleanup)
```
Any ideas for fixes or work-arounds? 
Thanks!

issue_title: Different results pydlm vs dlm package (in R)
issue_body:
I get different results in an extremely simple dynamic linear model, using dlm package in R and pydlm.
In particular, I create a random walk + noise dlm (that is dlm with just a trend of degree 0 or 1, depending on the version of the pydlm package). I fix the discount factor to be 1, that is W_t = 0 for all t (that is, the variance of the noise of the state evolution equation is null). The noisePrior is 1.0 by default. I fix the prior variance to be 2.0. I include just one observation: 1.70. With this I build the dlm:

Here the code:

from pydlm import *

d = dlm([1.70])
t = trend(degree = 0, discount=1, w = 2)
d = d + t
d.fitForwardFilter()
print(d.getLatentState())
[[1.1333333333333333]]
print(d.getLatentCov())
[matrix([[ 0.65444444]])]

Doing the same in R, I obtain the same latent state, but different state covariance. Particularly I obtain 0.666666.

In fact, performing the calculations by hand, I obtain the same as in R. This is quite easy to reproduce, following the formulas in page 53 of this book: Dynamic Linear Models with R,  Giovanni Petris, Sonia Petrone, Patrizia Campagnoli.

Am I misunderstanding the meaning of some parameter?

issue_title: Add '3.7' to _SUPPORTED_PYTHONS in 'jedi/jedi/api/environment.py' ?
issue_body:
Hello,

I use Elpy for code completion in Emacs. During recent switch to Python 3.7, I lost some of the code completion ability. It would complete everything from standard lib, but not from packages installed in virtualenvs.

I tracked this down to the list _SUPPORTED_PYTHONS from `jedi/jedi/api/environment.py` not having '3.7' in it. I have both 2.7.x and 3.7.0 on my system, and Jedi picks the system 2.7.x python binary and tries to run the Jedi `jedi/evaluate/compiled/subprocess/__main__.py` with it. 

After adding '3.7'  to the list in `api/environment.py`, I get back my completions for packages in the virtualenv, and the python3 binary from my virtualenv is used (as it should, as opposed to the system one.)

I am not familiar enough with the Jedi code-base to know if this is occurring due to a particular combination of a virtualenv with Python 3.7, Emacs 26.1, Elpy 1.22.0, Company 0.9.6, and Jedi 0.12.1.

I have gone through Elpy's python code and it correctly calls `jedi.Script()` with python3.7 from virtualenv, so I am guessing I have identified the issue correctly. But I may be wrong.

The reason I have some doubts is, I also tried out Company 0.9.6 with Anaconda-mode 0.1.12, and 
Jedi 0.12.1, and it was able to show correct code completion without adding '3.7'. I see that anaconda-mode makes a call to `jedi.create_environment()` to discover the virtualenv, and passes the Environment instance to `jedi.Script(..., environment=...)`. This seems missing from Elpy's `elpy/jedibackend.py`, but may be they do this in some other fashion?

Please advise if something else should be done. I am going to put a reference to this in Elpy issue tracker also, so they could also have a look at this.

    Only open files that exist are included. 
    
    Processes can have open files that have since been deleted and these are not 
    included in get_open_files. It would be useful if get_open_files could be 
    passed a variable with the option to include these files, as what is currently 
    returned does not match the list of open files that e.g. lsof returns.
    
    For example, this would be useful for determining which services need to be 
    restarted due to deleted files after package upgrades. Specifically, I was 
    attempting to rewrite the "checkrestart"[1] command using psutil instead of 
    lsof. It would greatly simplify the program if this functionality was present in psutil.
    
    [1] 
    http://anonscm.debian.org/gitweb/?p=collab-maint/debian-goodies.git;a=blob;f=checkrestart;h=6caaf109ba3a6ac764239fea01ddbc0425a6496d;hb=a8607224f80ac6630f358dc6b5bd9f1897e68896
    
    
_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/psutil/issues/detail?id=382_

issue_title: iproute.flush_route deletes more than asked
issue_body:
Hi Peter, long time to see !  :)

I've just hit the following bug in pyroute2:
```
>>> import pyroute2
>>> ipr = pyroute2.IPRoute()  # pylint: disable=no-member
>>> 
>>> for r in ipr.get_routes(table=254):
...    print r
... 
{'family': 2, 'dst_len': 24, 'proto': 2, 'tos': 0, 'event': 'RTM_NEWROUTE', 'header': {'pid': 6870, 'length': 60, 'flags': 2, 'error': None, 'type': 24, 'sequence_number': 261}, 'flags': 0, 'attrs': [('RTA_TABLE', 254), ('RTA_DST', '240.97.132.0'), ('RTA_PREFSRC', '240.97.132.200'), ('RTA_OIF', 374)], 'table': 254, 'src_len': 0, 'type': 1, 'scope': 253}
>>> ipr.flush_routes(family=pyroute2.common.AF_MPLS)
>>> for r in ipr.get_routes(table=254):
...        print r
... 
[nothing]
```

So, _ipr.flush_routes(family=pyroute2.common.AF_MPLS)_ deleted all the routes, the _family_ filter was not taken into account.  

I've observed the same issue with _proto_ as a filter to _flush_routes_.

I've had a look at the code, and nothing obvious stands out, flush_routes seems to rely on get_routes that *does* properly take into account kwargs to filter.

issue_title: Enable WHITENOISE_KEEP_ONLY_HASHED_FILES by default
issue_body:
WhiteNoise 4 adds a `WHITENOISE_KEEP_ONLY_HASHED_FILES` option, which causes the non-hashed files to be deleted. Given this is a major version bump release that's not yet come out of beta, I wondered whether the option should be set to `True` by default instead of the current `False` - so that WhiteNoise does the most efficient thing out of the box?

I was also thinking it would be good to upstream this behaviour - seems like there is a good chance it would be accepted:
https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27929

issue_title: Make Process Monitor Start Process Itself
issue_body:
Process monitor is able to re-start a process, but it will not work unless the process is already running when the test starts. The current workflow is:

1. Start the process under test.
2. Start the process monitor.
3. Start test.

issue_title: Fix registration of NumPy dtypes
issue_body:
In numpy-1.14, dtype field lookup has been changed to be  by position/offset rather than by field name.  If that version is installed, an `ImportError` gets raised when importing `fwdpy11` because dtype fields are declared in a different order than how they appear in the structs they reflect.

Another user of `pybind11` has run into this: pybind/pybind11#1274.

This should be fixed ASAP and a new release of 0.13 should follow.

cc @jeromekelleher

issue_title: support `responses.default`
issue_body:
According [to the responses spec](http://swagger.io/specification/#responsesObject), a `default` key should be used to validate any response codes not explicitly declared. Right now, though, flex will raise `invalid_status_code` without checking for the presence of `default`.

This should be an easy fix: the check for default can be done [here](https://github.com/pipermerriam/flex/blob/a8ddfae3393aea596b390022bb7263d2ca408937/flex/validation/response.py#L46).

issue_title: Wrong treatment of "big" double literals
issue_body:
I am facing a subtle problem when a big float literal is converted to infinity by Python. This is illustrated in the following snippet (tested with astor 0.5 on in Python 3.4):
```
print (astor.to_source(ast.parse('a = 1e400')))
# --> 'a = inf'
print (astor.to_source(ast.parse('a = -1e400')))
# --> 'a = (- inf)'
```
These round-trip calls are supposed to produce equivalent Python code that is syntactically valid. This is not the case here since `inf` is not a builtin object of Python. Actually, the bug occurs because the infinity value is already in the ast node; I assume that `astor.to_source` calls `repr` of this, which indeed returns `inf`. A satisfying solution for me could be this:
```
print (astor.to_source(ast.parse('a = 1e400')))
# --> 'a = float(inf)'
print (astor.to_source(ast.parse('a = -1e400')))
# --> 'a = - float(inf)'
```

issue_title: Installation error with python 3.4.0
issue_body:
```python
byte-compiling build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/_file_cache.py to _file_cache.cpython-34.pyc
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/_file_cache.py", line 65
    except (AttributeError, IOError, OSError), e:
                                             ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

byte-compiling build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/api.py to api.cpython-34.pyc
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/api.py", line 770
    print 'request_url', request_url, parameters
                      ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

byte-compiling build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/__init__.py to __init__.cpython-34.pyc
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/__init__.py", line 36
    raise ImportError, "Unable to load a json library"
                     ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

byte-compiling build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/twitter/list.py to list.cpython-34.pyc
```

for the first error we should have 

```python
    except (AttributeError, IOError, OSError) as e:
```
this instruction is not python 2.5 compatible ( http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2535760/python-try-except-comma-vs-as-in-except )

for the seconde we should have 

```python
    print ('request_url', request_url, parameters)
```
to keep compatibility with python < 3 you could use 

```python
from __future__ import (print_function)
```

for the last it should be 

```python
    raise ImportError("Unable to load a json library")
```

issue_title: Prevent email enumeration
issue_body:
The nice thing about only requiring an email and a password for registration is that you can prevent users from learning what emails are already used.  To do that, you can simply just say "We sent you an email, check your inbox" whether the provided email is taken or not. However, Flask-User doesn't hide that: it says "This Email is already in use. Please try another one." if an email is taken already.

That's often not an issue but I'd really like to be able to set an option like `USER_NO_EMAIL_ENUMERATION` so that Flask-User applies the trick I mentioned above. It's not really useful is you already ask for a username because those can be enumerated anyway, but when `USER_ENABLE_USERNAME = False` it would be nice to have that option.  What do you think?

issue_title: Profiles should input / output grids data structures
issue_body:
Currently, to compute an intensity / surface density / potential / deflection angle value at a set of coordinates, we can only pass an individual set of coordinates into the code, e.g.:

```
sersic = light_profiles.EllipticalSersic(intensity=1.0, effective_radius=0.6, sersic_index=4.0)
intensity = sersic.intensity_at_coordinates(np.array([1.0, 0.0]))
```

We are also restricted to individual coordinates if we do this via a Galaxy class:

```
sersic = light_profiles.EllipticalSersic(intensity=1.0, effective_radius=0.6, sersic_index=4.0)
galaxy = galaxy.Galaxy(redshift=0.5, light_profiles=[sersic])
intensity = galaxy.intensity_at_coordinates(np.array([1.0, 0.0]))
```

However, in many calculations, we don't pass one set of coordinates to a function but a 'grid', which is a NumPy array with some defined structure. For example, an image.grid is a NumPy array with dimensions ( image_grid[image_pixels, 2] ), such that each entry is a coordinate on the image grid. 

It'd be good if our _at_coordinates routines could take in an input numpy array of any shape, and output the results in a NumPy array following the same structure.

Currently, I have made a half-baked implementation of this by making separate routines for computing the values on a grid:

```
    def deflection_angles_grid(self, coordinates):

        deflection_angles = np.zeros(coordinates.shape)

        for defl_count, coordinate in enumerate(coordinates):
            deflection_angles[defl_count, :] = self.deflection_angles_at_coordinates(coordinates=coordinate)

        return deflection_angles
```

and on a sub grid:

```
    def deflection_angles_sub_grid(self, sub_coordinates):

        deflection_angles = np.zeros(sub_coordinates.shape)

        for sub_count, image_pixel in enumerate(sub_coordinates):
            for defl_count, sub_coordinate in enumerate(image_pixel):
                deflection_angles[sub_count, defl_count, :] = self.deflection_angles_at_coordinates(coordinates= sub_coordinate)

        return deflection_angles
```

It'd be good if we didn't need to set up separate functions for this. We could either make the functions generic for any NumPy array, or specific to the structures that our grids module uses (which at the moment is just the two above, image_grid[image_pixels, 2] and sub_grid[image_pixels, sub_grid_pixels, 2].

@rhayes777 When you're back, can your coding trickery easily set this up?

NOTE: For the intensity / surface_density / potential calculations, the input grid is in (x,y) coordinates, so always has size 2 in its last dimensions 2, e.g. image_grid[image_pixels, 2]. However, the output grid is not an (x,y) grid, so the dimensions output_grid[image_pixels]. In contrast, because deflection angles are (x,y) coordinates, the output grid is output_grid[image_pixels, 2]

issue_body:
I am using XlsxWriter to convert .mat Files into .xlsx Files.
If the .mat File only has singleprecision numbers which are in np.float32, XlsxWriter will write them as float64 and for that it appends non existing numbers to it.
Even if I convert the float32 to float64 before I write the Excelfile, it will write these non existing numbers again.
I am using Python version 2.7.3 and XlsxWriter 0.8.1 and Numpy 1.9.1

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:
```python
import xlsxwriter
import numpy as np

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx')

issue_title: possible issue with 0.7.10
issue_body:
I've upgraded to 0.7.10 to test the ngMessages work i've been doing but have come across an issue.

I have a base class that all my forms inherit from. It used to look like this for 0.7.3:
```
class BaseNgForm(NgMessagesMixin,
                 NgModelFormMixin,
                 NgFormValidationMixin,
                 Bootstrap3FormMixin):
        ...
```

After i'd updated to 0.7.10 i got this error

```AttributeError: 'EmailField' object has no attribute 'get_potential_errors'```

After a bit of poking around i could see that my fields weren't being converted to the djangular mixin fields and realised that in your example you use Bootstrap3Form as a base instead. So i changed to that:
```
class BaseNgForm(NgMessagesMixin,
                 NgModelFormMixin,
                 NgFormValidationMixin,
                 Bootstrap3Form):
```
But i'm still getting the same error.

I'm using all_auth and have their signin, signup and reset password forms all on the same page. Looking further into this i've found that the first form that gets processed is the reset form, which only has an EmailField. This get's processed ok by `NgFormValidationMixin.get_field_errors` as the `bound_field.field` is a `djangular.forms.angular_base.EmailField`.

However the next form it processes is signin, whose first field is also an `EmailField`. But even though this form also inherits from `BaseNgForm`, the `bound_field.field` is a `django.forms.fields.EmailField`.

I've checked through the example code to see if i'm missing something and have also read the docs, but can't see anything different. I've also tried to recreate this issue in the example by rendering 2 forms on the same page. But it doesn't occur.

Sorry for the small amount of info to work with, but i'm just wondering if there's anything that jumps out at you that might be causing this?

issue_title: Missing tag pair using chess.pgn.read_game() or chess.pgn.scan_headers()
issue_body:
According to [pgn standard spec](http://www.saremba.de/chessgml/standards/pgn/pgn-complete.htm#c8.1) tag pair names can contain underscores, but python-chess pgn parser ignores such names.

```
[Event "Local event"]
[Site "London"]
[Date "2000.01.01"]
[Round "1"]
[White "First Player"]
[Black "Second Player"]
[Result "1/2-1/2"]
[1Underscored_Tag "tag value"]

1. e4 c5 1/2-1/2
```

Telling the truth I'v never met any .pgn containing nor digits nor underscores in any tag, but the standard enables it, so it's better to handle it.

issue_title: needs to be made thread safe
issue_body:
I notice there are a number of places in watchdog that arn't properly thread safe. For example:

  EventDispatcher._dispatch_events()
        event, watch = event_queue.get(block=True, timeout=timeout)
        self.dispatch_event(event, watch)

This code path is not thread safe, as the watch object could get removed by the time the "dispatch_event" method obtains it's lock.

When running into this, it results in a KeyError and the resulting thread dying, thus watchdog no longer monitors a path. I had a traceback of this, but I've misplaced it at the moment, will past it it the next time I see it.

Thanks for your work on genetic programming. It's awesome and really easy to understand and use.

I'll be rather happy to help you improve this fantastic tool. There are much more can be added, things like selection function, mutation function, even user defined selection function, mutation function etc.

issue_title: _fileobject.read() is pretty badly broken
issue_body:
There are several significant issues here:

1. Depending on timing, when read() is called and the remote end terminates its SSL connection cleanly, SSLZeroReturnError will be raised to the user and the final bytes of the stream are lost forever.

    ```
                data = _safe_ssl_call(False, self._sock, 'recv', maxbufsize)
                if not data:
                    break
```

    In the above snippet, the second line will not execute when SSLZeroReturnError is raised.  `data` is a local variable and its contents are lost.

1. There are identical code patterns which need fixing in _fileobject.readline() as well (maybe other places too, I'm not sure).

1. In the case size < 0, read() does not keep the contract of reading until EOF.

    ```
        if size < 0:
            # Read until EOF
            self._rbuf = BytesIO()  # reset _rbuf.  we consume it via buf.
            data = _safe_ssl_call(False, self._sock, 'recv', rbufsize)
            buf.write(data)
            return buf.getvalue()
```

   In the above snippet, the comment is mistaken.  There would have to be a loop for it to be true.  No more than rbufsize additional bytes are read from the channel.

I'm trying to get some more debugging information out of assh. I'm having some weird issues when generating `~/.ssh/config` and am not sure why they are occurring.

I've tried several combinations of the `-l` flag and `--verbose`, and both at the same time:

    assh build -c ~/.ssh/config.test -c ~/.ssh/config.test --verbose --log_level=DEBUG

However, I never get any debugging or verbose output.

My `~/.ssh/config.advanced` file currently looks like:
```
[default]
User = $USER
Port = 22
PubkeyAuthentication = yes
IdentityFile = ~/.ssh/id_rsa
Includes = test_hosts/**/config.advanced
```

And ~/.ssh/test_hosts/example01/config.advanced
```
[example01]
hostname = host1.example.com
user = user01
Port = 2222
IdentityFile = ~/.ssh/id_rsa
```

And `~/.ssh/test_hosts/example02/config.advanced`:
```
[example02]
hostname = host2.example.com
user = user02
Port = 4444
IdentityFile = ~/.ssh/id_rsa
```

Running `assh build -c ~/.ssh/config.test -c ~/.ssh/config.test --verbose --log_level=DEBUG` results in the following `~/.ssh/config.test` file:

```
# assh version: 1.1.0

Host example01
  identityfile ~/.ssh/id_rsa
  port 2222
  user user01
  # hostname host1.example.com

Host example02
  identityfile ~/.ssh/id_rsa
  port 4444
  user user02
  # hostname host2.example.com

Host *
  identityfile ~/.ssh/id_rsa
  port 22
  proxycommand assh connect %h --port=%p
  pubkeyauthentication yes
  user $USER
```

- The hostname from each included file is commented out.
- The $USER value from `~/.config.advanced` `[default]` is not properly inserted.

On a similar but unrelated note - unless the first line of `~/.ssh/config` is `# assh version: 1.1.0` (regardless of whether we are rebuilding that file or a separate file) the build command will produce an error and refuse to generate the configuration file (unless the `-f` flag is provided). I didn't see any information in the documentation on this and it took me awhile to figure out.

issue_title: JSONType on MySQL can be suspect to silent value truncation
issue_body:
I just learned this the hard way, but on MySQL (at least when not in [Strict SQL Mode](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.0/en/sql-mode.html#sql-mode-strict)), columns of type ``TEXT`` will silently truncate values, thus corrupting the JSON representation:

> If strict SQL mode is not enabled and you assign a value to a BLOB or TEXT column that exceeds the column's maximum length, the value is truncated to fit and a warning is generated. For truncation of nonspace characters, you can cause an error to occur (rather than a warning) and suppress insertion of the value by using strict SQL mode. See Section 5.1.7, “Server SQL Modes”.

(https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.0/en/blob.html)

My personal workaround was to change the column type to ``LONGTEXT``, but that of course is only postponing the problem (to a length of 2^32, which is a lot, but still).

Hello, I'm probably doing something wrong, but I can't seem to create a new issue with a parent issue. First I'm creating the parent issue:

parent_issue = redmine.issue.create(project_id=project_identifier,

child_issue = redmine.issue.create(

Before knowing `pythran`, I only really paid attention to `cython` and `numba`. I chose `cython` over `numba` because cython is very portable. I just need to include c++ cythonized files to [my package](https://github.com/pytraj/pytraj) without bothering about installing LLVM  and other dependent stuff.  And cython works really well with C++ class.

Later I know `pythran` when watching your talk (youtube). From reading your papers, it seems that `pythran` dev really want to focus on `openmp` (and simd too?) with more control on how openmp work (similiar to c/c++); which is very great bonus.

so,

* How `pythran` can survive among many other compilers? 
* how `pythran` can support calling c++ class like `cython` (great thing in cython)
* it's likely that `pythran` heavily depend on `boost` and `nt2`, which require `conda` to manage the installation. How `pythran` can "beat" `numba`? :D 
* is there any intention to speeding up compiling code? Linking cythonzied code to c++ lib is quite slow but compiling `.so` file in `pythran` is even slower.

Hai

issue_title: Pull #83 cause tests to fail on Python3.x
issue_body:
As part of adding `ntohu64` to `parser.py`, the way byte order is fixed was changed.
Instead of a simple series of shifts into a new value, the code now create an array object and convert it to string after flipping the bytes.
This cause a `TypeError` on Python3.x when calling `struct.unpack()` which expect a byte-like.

To fix this the array should be converted to bytes instead string, or use the previous way of flipping the values, or any other way that return a byte-like to `struct.unpack()`.

This problem is released in v0.1.9.

Is there a feature to kill all the Tensorflow processes, including the ps worker so that the cluster can be reused?

issue_title: KubernetesJobTask bug: Pod status does not have conditions
issue_body:
I have several dozens of `KubernetesJobTask`s every day. And approximately twice a day this error happens:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 203, in run
    new_deps = self._run_get_new_deps()
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 140, in _run_get_new_deps
    task_gen = self.task.run()
  File "/usr/src/app/phpipeline/luigi_util.py", line 218, in run
    super(PHKubernetesJobTask, self).run()
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/contrib/kubernetes.py", line 353, in run
    self.__track_job()
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/contrib/kubernetes.py", line 199, in __track_job
    while not self.__verify_job_has_started():
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/contrib/kubernetes.py", line 276, in __verify_job_has_started
    for cond in status['conditions']:
KeyError: 'conditions'
```
Unfortunately, I cannot reproduce it.

But the problem is in the way status conditions are accessed:

- how it's done in `luigi`: `status['conditions']` ([line 276](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/38a1d0c4f95a1900e49b64d77cdb90078adc0416/luigi/contrib/kubernetes.py#L276)).
- how it's done in `pykube`: `status.get('conditions', [])` ([line 329](https://github.com/kelproject/pykube/blob/e8a46298a592ad9037587afb707ac75b3114eff9/pykube/objects.py#L329)).

I guess, it can be easily fixed by using the same way of getting status conditions, as in `pykube`.

issue_title: BaseHadoopJobTask always turns off reducers for hadoop jar tasks
issue_body:
`BaseHadoopJobTask.jobconfs` sets "mapred.reduce.tasks=0" if `reducer == NotImplemented`. Subclasses of `HadoopJarJobTask` would usually never override `reducer`, since the reducer is defined in the jar that it executes.

A hacky fix for this would be to set `reducer = None` in `HadoopJarJobTask`. Probably better would be to split up `BaseHadoopJobTask`, with one class providing generic jobconfs and the like, and `PythonHadoopJobTask` and a `JavaHadoopJobTask` classes that extend it.

Thoughts?

issue_title: DynamoDB expression objects do not properly support equality checks.

issue_title: RefreshableCredentials serializing without timezone info
issue_body:
Hey boto folks -

I'm currently implementing my own CredentialsProvider that provides RefreshableCredentials. I'm having issues with code that has been working, but recently broke. I have to wonder if either one of my dependencies got bumped (although I can't prove it) or because we just entered daylight savings.

Basically, I'm always hitting this condition: 

> Credentials were refreshed, but the refreshed credentials are still expired.

To create my meta-data, I'm using the same(?) code used in botocore:
`
In [1]: from datetime import datetime, timedelta
In [2]: from dateutil.tz import tzlocal
In [3]: expiration = datetime.now(tzlocal()) + timedelta(minutes=5)
In [4]: expiration.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')
Out[1]: '2016-04-07T10:26:04Z'
`
This doesn't encode my local time zone information, as %Z is needed.

I can demonstrate this inconsistency using some of the internal boto funcs
`
from botocore.credentials import _local_now, _serialize_if_needed, _parse_if_needed


now = _local_now()
print now

serialized = _serialize_if_needed(now)
print serialized

deserialized = _parse_if_needed(serialized)
print deserialized

print now - deserialized
`

> 2016-04-07 10:24:12.336556-04:00
> 2016-04-07T10:24:12Z
> 2016-04-07 10:24:12+00:00
> 4:00:00.336556

I can fix this issue on my end by encoding the timezone information in my format string, but I'm convinced botocore should do the same in its default `time_fetcher`

TL;DR
It seems like your `time_fetcher` is using a timezone aware (tzlocal) datetime, but you're losing the timezone information upon serialization, defaulting to UTC. So in my case, it's comparing EST to UTC when determining expiration time.

issue_title: Chrome Preferences not passed to Selenium Driver
issue_body:
After trying to disable images using splinter, I discovered that the options are not properly passed to the Selenium Driver. Using the Selenium Chrome Driver works without an issue.

Specifically the experemental options are treated in an non-standard way by chrome.
Fortunatelly the issue is easy to fix.

File: /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/splinter/driver/webdriver/chrome.py

OLD-with-Bug, line 17-20:

```
def __init__(self, user_agent=None, wait_time=2, fullscreen=False,
                 **kwargs):

        options = Options()
```

NEW-Bugfix, line 17-20:

```
def __init__(self, user_agent=None, wait_time=2, fullscreen=False, options=None,
                 **kwargs):

        options = options or Options()
```

Could you please add this fix to the main development ? Thanks a lot.

issue_title: Adding files to repository that is located directly in the root
issue_body:
This is a weird scenario, but if you init git repo in the root directory(e.g. D:\\) and try to add a file using gitpython, it will throw a ValueError:
```
Absolute path 'D:\\file' is not in git repository at 'D:\\'
```
The reason for this is improper validation in git/index/base.py _to_relative_path method:
```
    def _to_relative_path(self, path):
        """:return: Version of path relative to our git directory or raise ValueError
        if it is not within our git direcotory"""
        if not osp.isabs(path):
            return path
        if self.repo.bare:
            raise InvalidGitRepositoryError("require non-bare repository")
        relative_path = path.replace(self.repo.working_tree_dir + os.sep, "")
        if relative_path == path:
            raise ValueError("Absolute path %r is not in git repository at %r" % (path, self.repo.working_tree_dir))
        return relative_path
```
I.e. when path is '/file' and working_tree_dir is '/', relative_path stays equal to path, because `self.repo.working_tree + os.sep' is equal to `//`. The way to solve this is to simply use os.path.relpath methond. Please see pull-request attached.

PyHive fetchmany get stuck for sometime
issue_body:
Hi there,

We just encountered a very strange problem when using PyHive.

We use PyHive cursor to execute a Hive query and then fetch a fix number of rows from the result. This basic process is like:

```
...
cursor.execute(sql)
rows = cursor.fetchmany(num)
...
```

However, for the same query, there is a probability to get stuck at cursor.fetchmany(num) method (sometimes passed, sometimes stuck). We changed the num for some numbers and it still get stuck sometimes. 

Anyone can share some experience about this problem?

Thanks

Channing

issue_title: Unable to select nested iframe in an iframe
issue_body:
Current we are string a application that heavily uses iframes to get to the right elements we need to select an iframe and than select the second iframe. See the example below.
  <iframe id="A">
       ....
       <iframe id="B">
             ....
             <div id="C">   
             ....
       </iframe>
  </iframe>

When we do 
   Select Frame      //*[@id="A"]
   Select Frame      //*[@id="B"]       # Here the element cannot be found.
   Element Should Contain            //*[@id="C"]         Some text

The second Select Frame fails due it cannot be found.

Possible solution: add keyword for selecting a frame from with a frame

   Select Frame      //*[@id="A"] 
   Select Frame from Frame      //*[@id="A"]      //*[@id="B"]

The select frame from frame keyword should accept multiple frame ids to get the correct iframe.

issue_title: pipenv run fails when code contains PEP 484 type hints
issue_body:
My code contains the following snippit, which is valid syntax from python 3.6+

```py
from typing import List

NAMES: List[str] = []
```

When running the script via `pipenv run`,  I get a syntax error.

##### Describe your environment

1. Windows 10
1. Python version: Python 3.6.1
1. Pipenv version: pipenv, version 9.1.0

##### Expected result

Script runs correctly.

##### Actual result

```
$>pipenv run example.py
  File "<SNIP>\example.py", line 3
    NAMES: List[str] = []
         ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

Note that using `pipenv shell` then `python example.py` works correctly.

##### Steps to replicate

Create an empty 3.6 pipenv, and try to execute the following script:

```py
from typing import List

NAMES: List[str] = []

print("It works!")
```

issue_title: pipenv does not work in a pipenv shell
issue_body:
First, thanks for pipenv -- it's a great step forward in Python package management.  I'd like to roll it out for my team, but a few snags are preventing me.

I'd like to be able to run `pipenv shell` and have it work like a virtualenv; specifically, when I run `pipenv xxx` in that shell, from any directory, even outside the project directory, I expected it to operate on that virtualenv.  But alas.

Create a new empty pipenv:
```
$ mkdir proj
$ cd proj
$ pipenv install
Creating a virtualenv for this project…
Using /usr/bin/python (2.7.14) to create virtualenv…
⠋Already using interpreter /usr/bin/python
New python executable in /home/me/.venv/bin/python
Installing setuptools, pip, wheel...done.

Virtualenv location: /home/me/.venv/proj-9zRXrcHj
Creating a Pipfile for this project…
Pipfile.lock not found, creating…
Locking [dev-packages] dependencies…
Locking [packages] dependencies…
Updated Pipfile.lock (b44f36)!                                                                                              Installing dependencies from Pipfile.lock (b44f36)…
  🐍   ▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉▉ 0/0 — 00:00:00
To activate this project's virtualenv, run the following:
 $ pipenv shell
```

Activate that environment:
```
$ pipenv shell
Spawning environment shell (/bin/bash). Use 'exit' to leave.
$ . /home/me/.venv/proj-9zRXrcHj/bin/activate
```

Leave the project directory:
```
(proj-9zRXrcHj) $ cd ..
```

...And things fall apart:
```
(proj-9zRXrcHj) $ pipenv graph
Warning: Unable to display currently–installed dependency graph information here. Please run within a Pipenv project.
```

I expected that to work.

```
(proj-9zRXrcHj) $ pipenv install requests
Creating a virtualenv for this project…
^C
```

I expected that to install to the existing virtual environment (/home/me/.venv/proj-9zRXrcHj) instead of create a new one.

And I can't even provide debugging info, because pipenv does not install itself in the virtualenv:

```
(proj-9zRXrcHj) $ python -m pipenv.help
/home/me/.venv/proj-9zRXrcHj/bin/python: No module named pipenv
```

It's pipenv 11.9.0 on Redhat 7 with Python 2.7.14.

issue_title: modules statically linked to libpythonx.y.a are show as compliant

issue_title: Fetcher thread will cause high cpu usage when queues of partitions are full.
issue_body:
If queues of all owned partitions are full, no fetch requests will be generated. [This codes will be skipped](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/39bcf26f04f44c376c0f27074052504185dfa6f6/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L730-L733). And finally, `fetch` does nothing. So the fetcher thread will run `fetch` 10,000 times per second.
I don't know if it is your expected behavior.

Steps:
1. create a consumer with queued_max_messages=1.
2. start the consumer without consuming.
3. send messages to make all queues full.
4. run top to monitor cpu usage.

issue_title: deprecate SimpleConsumer's consumer_id parameter
issue_body:
According to the Kafka protocol documentation, `consumer_id` is invalid on group requests from non-managed consumers.

> Note that when this API is used for a "simple consumer," which is not part of a consumer group, then the generationId must be set to -1 and the memberId must be empty (not null). Additionally, if there is an active consumer group with the same groupId, then the commit will be rejected (typically with an UNKNOWN_MEMBER_ID or ILLEGAL_GENERATION error).

That means that [the consumer_id parameter](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/a9dd498392a2f78682719f44c56229cfff486c30/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L76) on `SimpleConsumer` should not actually be allowed. It should be deprecated in the next release and removed in the next major version.

The reason this parameter exists at all is because it's [used](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/a9dd498392a2f78682719f44c56229cfff486c30/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L411) by `ManagedBalancedConsumer`. The interface should be adjusted to allow `ManagedBalancedConsumer` to set this value appropriately for its needs without making it part of the public interface.

Andrew Boot from the [google group thread](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pykafka-user/2cBD-nWP-4Y) has offered to work on a pull request for this.

issue_title: warn early against unicode arguments
issue_body:
pykafka doesn't support unicode, which is fine. If client code supplies unicode in the wrong place, the error will manifest itself as something like this:

  File "bolts/common/kafka.py", line 58, in process_batch
    producer.produce(msg, partition_key=partition_key)

error: argument for 's' must be a string
```
A similar error also causes metadata fetching to fail when a unicode topic name is supplied to `TopicDict.__getitem__` (via subscript access on `KafkaClient.topics`).

I think it's worth catching these cases immediately instead of allowing them to raise inscrutable exceptions on worker threads.

issue_title: Starting > 1 consumer hangs python process
issue_body:
Using `pykafka==1.1.0`, running against a single node Kafka `0.8.2.1` server, the following script hang and never exits:

```python
from pykafka import KafkaClient


client = KafkaClient('kafka:9092')

consumer1 = client.topics['test1'].get_simple_consumer()
consumer2 = client.topics['test2'].get_simple_consumer()

print 'Consumers made'
```

Running I see this:

```bash
$ python application consumertest.py
Consumers made
```

And the process seems to hang forever.  I have waited as long as 5 minutes and it never exits.

For some more evidence, if I change the consumer constructor to `get_simple_consumer(auto_start=False)` the script exits, however, if I then add this code below...

```python
consumer1.start()
consumer2.start()
```

...the process hangs again, leaving me to think somewhere in the `consumer.start()` process is where the issue is.

I'm about to start digging into the `start()` code to see if I can track down the issue myself, but I wanted to post this here in case this is a known issue (I couldn't find any) or if you guys had a better idea what might be going on here?

issue_title: packagename.__version__ has wrong git info when run in git repository
issue_body: To reproduce, cd into any git repository and import a *different* astropy affiliated package. If the affiliated package is on a development version, `packagename.__version__` will be "MAJOR.MINOR.devNUM" where "NUM" is wrong: it is the number of commits in the current git repository rather than that of `packagename`.

issue_title: Let travis CI build and error-check the documentation
issue_body:
Currently, there is nothing but careful manual inspection stopping errors in the docs from being merged. This has led to problems: #665, #678, #212 (and there are probably more). The natural solution is to have travis CI check that the docs build cleanly.

Some challenges to keep in mind while working on this:

* sphinx can accept quite a lot of errors and still exit with a zero exit code: a decision on "how bad" things should be before we consider the doc build "failed" must be made, and then implemented
* if one of the examples fail to run under sphinx, it will be silently dropped from the example gallery (and some special circumstances can cause examples to fail under sphinx while working fine when run idividually, related to `__future__` imports)


The docs do not yet build on python3: #651. Still, error checking of the docs would be useful to have in place for python2 alonem which should be the first goal.

issue_title: exposure.equalize_hist doesn't output the same type as the input
issue_body:
I am not completely sure if it should be a feature request or a bug.
Currently exposure.equalize_hist given an image of input of type uint8 outputs an image of type float64. The proposal is to give the output in uint8 format itself. 
Current implementation:
```
img1 = io.imread(path_to_file)
img11 = skimage.exposure.equalize_hist(img1)
print('Original Image is of dtype ', img1.dtype)
print('Histogram Equalized Image is of dtype', img11.dtype)
```
Output:
```
('Original Image is of dtype ', dtype('uint8'))
('Histogram Equalized Image is of dtype', dtype('float64'))
```
#1192 mentions this, but the issue is not really addressed.
To be honest I am not even sure why the output image is of type 'float64'. At no point of time should  image of type uint8 should be changed to float64, i.e. there should be no case where we should be working with floats, we should be in integer domain itself. Here is a nice text describing histogram equalization https://www.math.uci.edu/icamp/courses/math77c/demos/hist_eq.pdf

Histogram equalization would simply be a transformation curve, mapping one intensity to another corresponding intensity (the formulae given in the mentioned link) and it should be all in the integer domain when the input is of type uint8. 

Also as far as I know, MATLAB also does the same thing, that is given an input of uint8 returns output in uint8 itself. Link for the same https://www.mathworks.com/help/images/ref/histeq.html

In short, the current implementation of the equalize_hist is doing something similar but will give a different result from what a normal histogram equalization would return at least for an uint8 image. Also I think it is more sensible to return image of the same dtype.

issue_title: Predicted field is not an integer when using coordinate encoder
issue_body:
I found this issue when trying to get https://github.com/numenta/nupic.geospatial running with NuPIC 1.0. 

Looks like this is the origin of the bug:

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/commit/34dd874896b22da217c5bede494f910faf6efb99#diff-d62e7e036bc450e0076245d0839b7ef4R381

In this commit, we start using `self.predictedField` as a condition to get encoder outputs instead of `self.predictedFieldIdx`. The CoordinateEncoder's `predictedFieldIdx` is always `-1`, so it worked before the change. But the CoordinateEncoders' `predictedField` is not `None`, it is `vector`, so the compute function tries to get bucket values from the encoder that do not exist. 

We need to add a special condition to the compute function to check also for `self.predictedField == "vector"`.

issue_title: Create an option NTA_SERIALIZATION_ON to disable serialization on Windows and others
issue_body:
As CapnProto is a very recent tool, many platforms/compilers are presenting build problems when you try link it to some project. Windows/MSVC is one of them (why I'm not surprise..). One of the reasons it's because many compilers doesn't have c++11 features fully implemented such as `constexpr` (https://github.com/kentonv/capnproto/blob/master/doc/_posts/2014-12-15-capnproto-0.5-generics-msvc-java-csharp.md):

>Not all features are supported yet. The core serialization functionality sufficient for 90% of users is available, but reflection and RPC APIs are not. We will turn on these APIs as soon as Visual C++ is ready (the main blocker is incomplete constexpr support).

So we have 2 choices:
- Implement a NTA_SERIALIZATION_ON pre-processor flag to isolate capn headers/libraries from build. Once capn works 100% in most environments, we remove this flag.
- Just wait to this problem be solved by capn team (maybe in a few days, maybe in a few months).

issue_title: SP Save/load causes duty cycle variations, which cause boost factor variations
issue_body:
The prototest is failing.

~~~
ASSERTION FAILED: "outputBaseline[i] == outputC[i]"
~~~

It works fine for the capnproto `read` / `write`, but it fails for the older `save` /  `load`.

I debugged it, and I saw that serializing and deserializing the SP causes the columns' active duty cycles to start to diverge. Eventually they become large enough to cause a boost factor variation, where some columns have a boost factor of 0.99 and others of 1.01. This causes different columns to get activated.

I don't recommend this change, but the test starts passing when we change this line https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/3e71bb596432d1c3137c02d5c79c7d638efceb66/src/nupic/algorithms/SpatialPooler.cpp#L1156

to

~~~c++
    Real activeDutyCycle = round(activeDutyCycles_[i] * 1000.0) / 1000.0;
    Real boostFactor = exp((targetDensity - activeDutyCycle)* maxBoost_);
~~~

issue_title: Blosc compression not widely supported
issue_body:
The blosc compression (provided by the PyTables backend) is a pretty clear winner:

* http://deepdish.readthedocs.org/en/latest/io.html#sparse-matrices

However, it is not widely supported, so deepdish-saved files won't be loadable through for instance Matlab or h5py. This severely damages the otherwise great portability of HDF5 and can especially be confusing for new users.

I am considering the following solution:

* Make zlib the default compression method. This is much more widely supported (both Matlab and h5py support it, as an example). The compression rate is even slightly better than blosc, but unfortunately it is much slower.
* Add a deepdish config file (maybe `.deepdish.conf` or `.ddrc`) where the default compression can be changed. That way, users that care more about speed than portability can opt-in to blosc without having to specify it every time.

issue_title: Improve documentation about transports
issue_body:
I ran into a lot of trouble with this, so I'm sure other people must have, too.  I can't tell if it's a documentation problem, or a code problem, or both.  I was able to fix my setup with just changes to my code and configuration, though.

Background: I need to use a synchronous transport (because my application's process will exit shortly after an exception that I want to report to Sentry), so when I heard that Sentry used an async transport by default, I went and read this document: http://raven.readthedocs.org/en/latest/transports/index.html

First of all, the sentence "By default, Raven uses a threaded asynchronous transport, but you can easily adjust this by modifying your SENTRY_DSN value." is a little confusing.  It makes it sound like the DSN is what selects the transport.  But then two lines down when it shows some sample code, the DSN doesn't say what transport to use.  The "transport=" keyword parameter does.  But maybe the strange wording here was intentional.  I'll come back to this below.

When I try something like this:

    import raven
    s = raven.Client(dsn='https://FOO:BAR@app.getsentry.com/BAZ',
                     transport=raven.transport.HTTPTransport)
    try:
        1/0
    except:
        s.captureException()

it doesn't show up in the Sentry dashboard.  Similarly for raver.transport.RequestsHTTPTransport.  (It returns an event_id without error, but after a bit of searching I discovered that the event_id is created on the client-side, so this was not an indication of success.)

Upon digging through the logs, I discovered that it was failing here: https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/transport/base.py#L29

It turns out that each Transport class has its own "scheme" list, and it will refuse to even try to send an event unless your store_endpoint has a protocol in that list.  ThreadedHTTPTransport (the default) has ['http', 'https', 'threaded+http', 'threaded+https'].  HTTPTransport's is ['sync+http', 'sync+https'], and RequestsHTTPTransport's is ['requests+http', 'requests+https'].  Internally, it builds the store_endpoint from the DSN you provide, with the same protocol.

This, combined with the strange sentence at the top (which suggested that the DSN needed modification in order to pick a different Transport) led me to figure out how to get it working with other transports.  For example, to use the RequestsHTTPTransport, I had to:

 1. pass transport=raven.transport.RequestsHTTPTransport to the Client initializer, and also
 2. change my DSN from "https" (as getsentry.com had told me) to "requests+https".

Then everything works!  But this was not at all easy to figure out.

By the way, this also means that the sample code on page <http://raven.readthedocs.org/en/latest/transports/index.html> won't work.  It asks for transport=HTTPTransport, but the DSN URL starts with "http", when it should start with "sync+http".

Alternatively, I suppose, you could just remove check_scheme.  I'm not sure why it exists.

issue_title: Tornado transport should handle exceptions
issue_body:
If an error occurs while logging to sentry (for example rate limiting), Tornado (at least in recent versions) will complain about a future error which has not been retrieved. It will log the error to the Tornado logger, and if you have the SentryHandler configured for your loggers then you'll get a nice loop with logging going crazy.

The AsyncSentryClient and the TornadoHTTPTransport should run IOLoop.add_future with a callback that runs future.result() to retrieve any error, and print the error to stdout.

issue_title: Implement watchdog support
issue_body:
When Patroni dies or wedges itself split brain protection is not guaranteed. Adding watchdog support would give reasonably good guarantees of PostgreSQL master going away when Patroni dies. Watchdog driver will ensure that the server gets rebooted when the heartbeat stops.

For now I'm researching two implementation avenues:
1. Create check script and example config for [watchdog daemon](http://www.sat.dundee.ac.uk/psc/watchdog/Linux-Watchdog.html).
2. Use [Linux kernel watchdog API](https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/watchdog/watchdog-api.txt) directly.

The daemon option is probably more fully featured and integrates better with other liveness checks. Using the kernel API directly is simpler, avoids an external dependency and integration with Patroni lifecycle is simpler (initialize watchdog when Patroni starts, ping watchdog once per loop, close watchdog when gracefully exiting).

Is this wanted? Any preference for which way to go about this?

Patch coming up soon.

issue_title: Comportement anormal du revenu_super_brut sur Décembre 2017
issue_body:
Mail reçu sur contact :
Date : 7 novembre
Objet : Comportement anormal du simulateur sur Décembre 2017
``` 
Bonjour,

Je vous contacte à propos de l'API OpenFisca utilisée pour une simulation de salaire. 
Nous avons constaté aujourd'hui que renseigner le mois de décembre 2017 dans la date de début d'embauche entraînait une réponse qui semble erronées, de l'ordre de plusieurs milliers d'euros.

Nous avons reproduit ce comportement sur le simulateur embauche.beta.gouv.fr.

Ci dessous un premier appel (celui avec les paramètres par défaut) sur une simulation d'embauche en novembre 2017 :

issue_title: linecache2 does not conform to PEP 396 (missing __version__ string)
issue_body:
For our package management we use automated tooling to extract version numbers from python packages. I would like to request that you add the `__version__` attribute version string.

[PEP 396](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0396/) states:

* On a case-by-case basis, standard library modules which are also released in standalone form for other Python versions MAY include a module version number when included in the standard library, and SHOULD include a version number when packaged separately.
* When a module (or package) includes a version number, the version SHOULD be available in the `__version__` attribute.
* The `__version__` attribute's value SHOULD be a string.

issue_title: Invalid syntax in inspect_fodder2.py
issue_body:
As pointed out, in [#1](https://github.com/testing-cabal/linecache2/issues/1) this install fine - apologies for misinterpreting the output. However we're using a complex toolchain (https://github.com/stackforge/anvil) to automatically convert a large number of python packages and their dependencies into system packages. This toolchain blows up when it gets to linecache2 because the default RPM toolchain attempts to byte-compile *.py, and it finds a syntax error in inspect_fodder2.py.

Please could you consider renaming that file to have a different extension? Otherwise we'll need to add a special case patch to our build, which is painful. It would also make the install not have any warnings, which is often considered desirable :-)

issue_title: num_retries for all requests like googleapiclient
issue_body:
I am confused about the `num_retries` differences between the old `googleapiclient` and the new `google-cloud-python`.

I am able to specify `num_retries` for all API requests with the old `googleapiclient` by either

 1. specifying `num_retries` for all requests
 2. or supplying own `requestBuilder` for the `googleapiclient.discovery.build` which subclasses the original `googleapiclient.http.HttpRequest` and sets `num_retries` for all API requests.

The new `google-cloud-python` allows `num_retries` just for the storage blob upload. Is there other way to specify `num_retries` for other API calls? I think that all API calls (including Datastore, etc.) may fail by HTTP 500 or 429 and then they need to retry the requests, e.g. see https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/exponential-backoff

Also, the `googleapiclient.http._should_retry_response()` seems different than `google.cloud.streaming.http_wrapper.make_api_request()`. E.g. the old one examines the response JSON payload, too.

issue_title: release.sh broken by `check_native_engine`, only releasing native engine on tags
issue_body: In e96947877c294d2134051351186416d9b9c42c22 I added a `release.sh` check for a matching released native engine. This was at a time when native engine releases were performed for every commit landing on master with a native engine edit. In e0acb4f304c055fa6eb1ee7db9e909d369610305 I restricted native engine releases to tagged commits at the request of bintray folks (we were burning up too much disk space for an OSS free tier project). This leads to a chicked/egg. The tags are created by `release.sh` _after_ checks pass, but the `check_native_engine` check can now never pass as a result (assuming the release contains >0 native engine changes).

issue_title: import/export: drive listing improvements
issue_labels: [{'id': 492821376, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/DEV:%20frontend', 'name': 'DEV: frontend', 'color': 'DFDFDF', 'default': False}, {'id': 492816641, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20tech%20update', 'name': 'TASK: tech update', 'color': '996189', 'default': False}, {'id': 492816719, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20ux%20update', 'name': 'TASK: ux update', 'color': 'E2D1E0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
## Part 1 - drive listing

Currently, the single-drive view looks different from multiple-drives. We also completely hide drives that are deemed "not available" - i.e. drives that don't have data on import and or are not writable on export.

We've had feedback that all these special cases are confusing, and that it would be simpler to always use the same radio button UI:

 * All drives should be shown as radio buttons
 * Drives that are 'not available' should be disabled radio buttons. After the drive name, we should add text such as _(not writable)_ or _(no Kolibri data found)_ to indicate why they're disabled.
 * If only one drive is available, it should still be shown as a radio button.

## Part 2 - translation strings and pluralization

The import/export modal window don't currently use `$tr` wrapping, and therefore don't properly pluralize messaging. All user-facing strings should be wrapped and make use of pluralization directives as necessary.

See e.g.:
 * http://formatjs.io/guides/message-syntax/#plural-format
 * https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/plugins/coach_tools/assets/src/vue/reports/summary-section/index.vue#L115


## Part 3 - layout

Currently everything is center-aligned, which looks messy for lists of radio buttons and is inconsistent with other modal dialogs.

Let's take out out the center-alignment and make the text alignment consistent with other modals: left-aligned headers, and right-aligned buttons.

issue_title: Notorious form data issue
issue_body:
Currently, the way form data is stored is by combining all fields and values into a single string.
Each field/value combo is joined by a  ``:`` character and separated from the others via a newline.
This was an early not well thought out design decision because it means that any field that has a value with a ``:`` in it will cause Aldryn Forms to think that this value is it's own field.
This affects the importing and storing of form data as pointed out here https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-forms/blob/master/aldryn_forms/models.py#L395.

I propose that instead of storing all field/values together, we either use a json field (with ordering) or an array field with the field/value pairs. This should be fairly simple to implement but my concern is backwards compatibility, this has to be backwards compatible which means we need a data migration that uses existing logic to separate each field/value pair from the stored string and then store them in the new json or array field, sadly there's no 100% reliable way we can actually tell if a field in the string is valid because of the issue mentioned above, to make it more reliable we could verify each found field with the form plugin fields but this would still not be 100% reliable because the idea behind form data was that the form plugin can be deleted without affecting anything in the stored data.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Support extensible metadata for secrets
issue_body:
Using a [JSONAttribute](http://pynamodb.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#pynamodb.attributes.JSONAttribute) in pynamodb, support extensible metadata on secrets. The use-case here is to keep the Confidant server dumb, but allow Confidant clients to be intelligent. For instance, we could have metadata like:

```
mode: 0600
path: /etc/mysecret
user: root
group: root
```

The server is simply storing data, but when the client fetches the secret, it can decide what it wants to do with the secret. In this case it would place the file in /etc/mysecret only accessible by root.

This is partially motivated by https://github.com/lyft/confidant/issues/1.

issue_title: Passing host_config parameters in start() overrides the host_config that was passed in create()?
issue_body:
I had a `host_config` with `extra_hosts` defined. I used this `host_config` to create a container. When starting container, I passed extra volumes_from parameter. However, this lead to `extra_hosts` seemingly not doing their job. 

After I moved `volumes_from` from `start()` to `create_host_config` everything started working OK. 

I figured, I should report this, since I spent a couple of hours figuring this out. IMO, start() should override the necessary parts of host_config(and not the whole thing). Or raise an exception.

issue_title: We should test examples as part of system tests
issue_body:
Verifying examples work manually (read- we don't accidentally break them) is a hassle. We should integrate them to our nimi-bot testing.

First, the examples should be refactored so that they can be included – add `__name__`, main(), etc. similar to how [nixnet-python](https://github.com/ni/nixnet-python/blob/master/nixnet_examples/can_signal_conversion.py) does it.
(we should do this regardless).

Then we can have a single test file in each examples/ folder (note - this should not make it to documentation, will need filtering) that imports and invokes the examples in a way that they work on simulated device.

issue_title: some doubts
issue_body:
Hi All ,
 Thanks for making such a brilliant NLP  library , i have some doubts for a project , can you all tell me how to achieve that using Allen NLP or any of your methods ..

**PROBLEM STATEMENT** - i am trying to create a probability tool for creating scores  of importance eg (9/10, 8/10 or 6/10 ) depending on the  degree of importance ,from a book text corpus , i am trying to calculate the probability of certain topic coming in the exam using previous year question papers . i have around 10 yrs of question papers comprising of 300 question in total from  the same syllabus . but books are different .  since the questions are not straight forward ,  i am unable to do this 

**WHAT HAVE I TRIED** - i tried using topic modelling and  LDA method  in it ,but it gives only important topics or words from a text corpus . i was able to generate important topics from text , using GENSIM library but it couldn't  solve my problem of matching  and comparing it with the questions from the previous years question papers as some times questions were indirect or twisted . 

**WHAT I AM HOPING** -  i  am hoping is there a  way ALLEN NLP library can help me with understanding the indirect questions or twisted questions to generate a topic  which may help in matching it with the topics in book corpus from the same syllabus thus generating a probability of topics occuring in the exam using previous years   question papers . 

Looking forward for your help , any kind of help in this particular problem statement would br of great help to us 

Thanks

issue_body:

issue_title: Ignore http_session argument for local file
issue_body: At the moment Tabulator will complain if you supply http_session but load a local file not a url. This means that you need to determine externally if a path is a url or local and pass the http_session parameter depending upon that. It would be better if this was not necessary ie. that http_session is simply ignored for local files.

issue_title: Fix `globus update` invocation of `pip` to work on pip>=9.0.2
issue_body:
Okay, it turns out that we've been using `pip.main` because I mistakenly believed that this was a public, supported API.

It turns out not to be the case, and this is not supported usage.
It breaks on pip 9.0.2 and pip 10.x (beta now available) and we need to fix.

Full doc on what to do:
https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/user_guide/#using-pip-from-your-program

`pip` issue with the most detail and gnashing of teeth:
https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/5081

---

I think it would be lovely if `pip` itself implemented the `subprocess` logic, but I don't want to wait for us to fix this and start getting it out.
I'll put together and test a fix now, and then we can rebase #392 to use the changed version.

issue_title: JournalDB seems to make ChainDB.persist_trie_data_dict() at least 20x slower
issue_body:
`ChainDB.persist_trie_data_dict()` is yet another bottleneck I've found when running a full-node sync: it takes between 3 to 9 seconds to persist a batch of (128) bodies or (256) receipts, and most of that was spent on `JournalDB.set()`, which does a `wrapped_db.get()` before setting the new value.

This is specially problematic when performing a chain sync, but at the same a chain sync doesn't need a journaled DB, so maybe in that case we can just use a custom, non-journaled, DB class? Can anybody think of possible problems with that? Also, does that mean we'd need to extend trinity's DB-serving process to also offer a non-journaled chaindb?
